title: Email notifications arriving hours late
body: Task assignment notifications used to arrive within a minute or two. For the past week, they've been arriving 2-4 hours late. This is causing people to miss deadlines because they don't know they've been assigned a task until hours later. It seems to be worse in the morning — notifications sent around 9-10am take the longest to arrive. Afternoon notifications are usually faster but still delayed.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
Thanks for the detailed report — the timing pattern you noticed (worse at 9-10am, better in the afternoon) is a really useful clue.

To help narrow this down: is this delay affecting **all users** on your team/organization, or have you only confirmed it on your own account? And do you know if there were any deployments, configuration changes, or infrastructure updates to your TaskFlow instance around the time this started (~1 week ago)?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
Yeah, it's not just me — I've checked with a few people on my team and they're all seeing the same delays. So it's definitely not account-specific.

As for changes, I'm not aware of any infrastructure updates, but I did notice we started getting these new daily digest emails about a week ago — like a marketing/summary email thing. I don't know if that's related or just a coincidence with the timing. I don't have visibility into the deployment side of things though.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That daily digest email is a really interesting lead — the timing lines up almost exactly with when the delays started.

Do you know roughly what time those daily digest emails arrive in your inbox? Specifically, I'm wondering if they land around 9-10am — right when you're seeing the worst notification delays. If the digest is being sent to all users at once through the same email pipeline, it could be flooding the queue and pushing your transactional notifications to the back of the line.

comments.author: reporter
comments.body: Now that you mention it, yeah I think the digest emails do come in around 9am — or at least that's when I first started noticing them in my inbox. And we have around 200 people on our instance so if everyone's getting one of those... that could be a lot of emails going out at once. I hadn't really connected the two things but the timing does match up pretty well with when the delays are worst.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body:
That's a really compelling connection — 200 digest emails hitting the queue at 9am lines up perfectly with your worst delays.

One more thing that would help us nail this down: do you know what email service or infrastructure your TaskFlow instance uses to send notifications? For example, is it going through something like SendGrid, Amazon SES, a self-hosted SMTP server, or do you not have visibility into that? This matters because the fix (separate queues, rate limiting the digest, scheduling it off-peak) depends on what's under the hood.

Also — have you noticed whether the afternoon delays have been getting gradually worse over the past week, or have they stayed roughly consistent?

comments.author: reporter
comments.body:
I think we use some kind of SMTP relay but I'm not really sure about the specifics — that's more of an infrastructure thing that our admin handles. I don't have access to that side of things.

As for the afternoon delays, they've been pretty consistent I think. Like, mornings are always the worst and then it gets better as the day goes on. It hasn't seemed to be getting worse over the week, just the same pattern every day.

comments.author: triage-agent
comments.body: [RESOLVED] {"title":"Transactional email notifications delayed 2-4 hours, likely caused by daily digest emails saturating shared email queue","problem":"Task assignment email notifications that previously arrived within 1-2 minutes are now delayed by 2-4 hours. The delays are worst in the morning (9-10am) and improve through the afternoon. This has been happening consistently for approximately one week, affecting all ~200 users on the instance, causing missed deadlines due to late awareness of task assignments.","root_cause_hypothesis":"A new daily digest email feature, launched approximately one week ago, sends ~200 emails at approximately 9am through the same SMTP relay/queue used for transactional notifications. This bulk send saturates the email queue, pushing transactional notifications (task assignments, etc.) to the back of the line. The queue gradually drains throughout the day, explaining why afternoon notifications are faster but still somewhat delayed (residual queue depth).","reproduction_steps":["Wait for ~9am when the daily digest batch is sent to all ~200 users","Assign a task to a user in TaskFlow immediately after or during the digest send window","Observe that the assignment notification email arrives 2-4 hours late","Repeat the same task assignment in the afternoon (e.g., 3pm) and observe a shorter but still present delay","Compare against expected delivery time of 1-2 minutes"],"environment":"TaskFlow instance with ~200 users, using an SMTP relay (specific provider unknown — managed by infrastructure admin). Daily digest email feature recently enabled.","severity":"high","impact":"All ~200 users on the instance are affected. Users are missing task deadlines because they are not notified of assignments in a timely manner. The delay is worst during peak morning working hours when timely notification is most critical.","recommended_fix":"1. **Immediate investigation:** Check the email queue depth and processing logs around 9am to confirm the digest batch is causing the backlog. 2. **Separate queues:** Split transactional notifications (task assignments, mentions, etc.) and bulk/marketing emails (daily digest) into separate queues with independent throughput, prioritizing transactional emails. 3. **Rate-limit or stagger the digest:** Instead of sending all ~200 digests at once, drip them over a longer window (e.g., 50/minute) to avoid queue saturation. 4. **Schedule off-peak:** Move the digest send to an off-peak time (e.g., 6am or 7am) so the queue clears before the workday starts. 5. **Long-term:** Consider using a dedicated email service or separate sending infrastructure for bulk emails.","proposed_test_case":"After implementing queue separation or digest throttling: send 200 digest emails and simultaneously trigger 5 task assignment notifications. Verify that all 5 transactional notifications are delivered within 2 minutes, regardless of digest queue state. Repeat at 9am to confirm the fix holds during peak hours.","information_gaps":["Exact email infrastructure (SMTP provider, queue implementation, rate limits) — must be obtained from the infrastructure admin","Whether the digest feature has its own queue or shares the transactional notification queue — requires codebase/config inspection","Server-side email queue metrics (queue depth, processing rate, error rates) to quantitatively confirm the hypothesis","Whether any email rate limits from the SMTP provider are being hit, which could compound the issue"]}
